ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class; no specific yaki chicken udon with dashi broth entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific yaki chicken udon entry. A0EJW (Asian-style noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of yaki chicken udon with dashi broth is approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, grilled chicken pieces, vegetables, and dashi broth in a bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Yaki Udon (stir-fried udon noodles with chicken and vegetables) — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked udon noodles (FDC ID 168917), cooked chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), and dashi broth components, weighted to a representative restaurant-style bowl
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for yaki chicken udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked udon noodles (~50% by weight), cooked chicken breast (~25% by weight), dashi broth (~15% by weight), and mixed vegetables including cabbage, spring onions, and bean sprouts (~10% by weight). Soy sauce and mirin seasoning contributions to sodium and sugar are included. Weighted per-100g values are calculated from these proportions. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; udon noodles, boiled (GI ~55 referenced against glucose)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yaki chicken udon with dashi broth as a composite restaurant dish. GI of 55 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled udon noodles (GI ~55, glucose reference), which constitutes the dominant glycemic carbohydrate fraction of this dish. The addition of chicken, dashi broth, and vegetables does not substantially raise the GI; protein and fat co-ingestion may modestly lower the effective GI. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 60.75) / 100 ≈ 33.4, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 13.5 × 4.5 = 60.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi broth, soy sauce, and mirin seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 450g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, representing 82–94% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce, limit broth consumption, and consider this dish an occasional rather than regular meal choice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from udon noodles as the primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of ~33 per serving, this dish produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and account for the full carbohydrate load (~61g per bowl) within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1890 mg per serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is relatively low at ~1.8g per serving, and the lean chicken protein profile is broadly compatible with heart-healthy eating patterns when sodium is managed through reduced-sodium condiment choices.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yaki Chicken Udon with Dashi Broth is a Japanese noodle dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles stir-fried or grilled (yaki) with tender pieces of chicken, and served in or alongside a savoury dashi broth made from kombu seaweed and bonito flakes. The dish typically includes vegetables such as cabbage, spring onions, and bean sprouts, seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil. The texture is hearty and satisfying, with the noodles absorbing the umami-rich dashi broth, while the grilled chicken adds a slightly smoky, savoury flavour. Yaki udon is a popular comfort food in Japan and has become widely available in Japanese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, protein-containing meal (~98 kcal per 100g, ~7.8g protein per 100g) with udon noodles as the primary carbohydrate source. The main clinical concern is sodium content, which is elevated due to dashi broth, soy sauce, and mirin; a single restaurant serving can approach or exceed 1800–2000 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low-medium boundary), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for those with wheat or gluten intolerance, and contains fish allergens from dashi.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for yaki chicken udon with dashi broth as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked udon noodles, cooked chicken breast, dashi broth, and mixed vegetables, weighted by approximate proportions in a standard restaurant serving. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled udon noodles. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct dish-level measurement in any primary approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (cooked udon noodles FDC 168917, cooked chicken breast FDC 171477, dashi broth components, mixed vegetables) weighted to approximate restaurant-style proportions. No direct dish-level measurement in an approved primary source was available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the component sources used for this composite estimate. 5. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lean chicken and udon noodle components per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero by component source data. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled udon noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). 7. The food_name in the input record contained a trailing parenthesis ('606D Yaki Chicken Udon with Dashi Broth(') which appears to be a data entry artefact; the canonical name has been normalised to 'Yaki Chicken Udon with Dashi Broth'. The menu code '606D' is preserved in aliases. 8. Halal status is listed as false as dashi broth contains bonito (fish) which is generally permissible, but the dish may contain mirin (rice wine with alcohol) which renders it non-halal under strict interpretation; restaurant-specific verification is required. 9. Serving size of 450g is estimated based on typical Japanese restaurant udon bowl portions.
